# ai4coding \@ WB : Building right context to effective analysis

*Eduard Bukin — April 2026*

## The demand is real

In February 2026, the World Bank's Distributional Impact of Policies
Unit launched an event series aimed at Revolutionizing Stata (and R/Python)
coding workflow among economists by using generative AI agents and
GitHub Copilot. With the first seminar we aimed at introducing the
key concept of AI-Assisted coding, such as Integrated Development
Environments (IDEs)


CopilotJustndefined" seminar on AI-assisted coding with Positron, the
new data science IDE from Posit. The response was immediate: colleagues
across units asked for more. That seminar led directly to a full two-day
course in April 2026 — not proposed top-down, but demanded by staff.
Across both events, XXX unique participants joined, ranging from
experienced Stata analysts to R and Python users. The common thread was
clear: everyone wanted to move beyond pasting code into a browser
chatbot and start using AI where it actually matters — inside their
development environment.

This post distills the core argument from that course: adopting AI for
coding is not just about having access to a large language model. It is
about embedding AI into the right tools so it can see your workspace,
understand your data, execute your code, and iterate autonomously.

## What the course taught: from chat to agentic IDE

The course followed a deliberate progression. Day 1 covered the
foundations: setting up Positron with Stata, R, and Python; configuring
GitHub Copilot access; understanding how large language models work —
token prediction, context windows, and why hallucinations happen.
Participants wrote their first AI-assisted code edits and learned basic
prompting techniques.

Day 2 moved to advanced features: tools and the Model Context Protocol
(MCP), custom instructions and prompt files, autonomous agents, skills
files, and planning agents. A recurring theme was version control with
Git — not as a side topic, but as an essential practice when an
autonomous agent is editing your files on your behalf.

The core lesson across both days was this: there is a fundamental
difference between asking AI a question in a browser window and giving
AI access to your workspace, your data, and your execution environment.
The first is convenient. The second is transformative.

## Why the IDE is the multiplier

### Chat-based AI works blind

When you paste a code snippet into ChatGPT or Claude in a browser, the
model sees only what you give it. It has no access to your files, your
data dictionaries, your folder structure, or your Stata installation. It
cannot run your code. It cannot observe the error message that comes
back. It certainly cannot fix the error and try again.

This limitation matters. In a 2023 World Bank blog post, Owen Ozier
tested ChatGPT on six Stata coding tasks. The results were mixed at
best. The model hallucinated a fictitious `pwvariance` function, blended
SAS and Stata syntax, and misinterpreted a standard regression
specification. It produced working recursive Fibonacci code in R,
Python, Matlab, and C — but not in Stata. Ozier attributed this partly
to Stata's smaller internet footprint: less public code means less
training data.

### IDE integration changes the equation

Three years later, the picture has changed — but not primarily because
models got smarter. In an April 2026 blog post, Visintini, Jones, and
Singh from DIME ran current AI tools on a past version of their research
assistant hiring test. Without an execution environment, GitHub Copilot
scored roughly 50% in both R and Stata — approximately the level of a
median human applicant. With an agentic setup — Claude Code or OpenAI's
Codex running inside Positron — scores jumped to approximately 80% in
both languages.

The gap between 50% and 80% is not a model upgrade. It is an environment
upgrade. When the AI agent can read data files, execute code, observe
errors, and iterate, it closes the gap that blind code generation
cannot.

### What IDE integration unlocks

An AI-capable IDE provides several capabilities that a browser chat does
not:

- **Context integration.** The AI reads your entire workspace — scripts,
  data dictionaries, configuration files — not just the snippet you
  paste in. It understands the project structure.
- **Secure workspace access.** The AI operates within a sandboxed
  project folder through controlled tools. It does not have arbitrary
  access to your system.
- **Inline assistance.** Completions, suggestions, and edits happen
  directly in your code. There is no copy-paste cycle between a browser
  tab and your editor.
- **Autonomous agents.** This is the critical capability. The AI
  executes your code, reads the output or error, adjusts, and tries
  again — repeating until the objective is met or it asks for guidance.
  During the course, participants watched an agent run an entire Stata
  do-file, encounter path errors, fix them, re-execute, and produce
  correct output — all within Positron, all reviewable through Git
  diffs.

### Niche languages are no longer a barrier

The early concern — that Stata's small public footprint would
permanently disadvantage AI assistance in that language — has not held.
The technology has shifted. Skills files and custom instructions allow
users to preload domain-specific knowledge into an agent. The DIME
Analytics coding standards, for example, can be loaded as a skill,
causing the agent to follow style conventions it was never trained on.
The Stata MCP server gives the agent direct access to the Stata runtime,
enabling it to execute and debug `.do` files natively.

The Visintini et al. results confirm this: once the agent has an
execution environment, Stata and R scores are nearly identical. The
pattern generalizes. Any niche tool — any proprietary language, any
specialized data format — can be made AI-accessible through context
engineering.

## The new skill set: from coder to agent manager

The most important shift the course aimed to convey is not technical. It
is about workflow. The traditional workflow is: I write code, I run it,
I read the error, I fix it. The AI-assisted workflow is: I specify the
objective, the agent writes and runs the code, I review the output and
diffs, I steer.

This is a genuine change in how analytical work gets done. It does not
eliminate the need for coding knowledge — it redirects where that
knowledge matters most. The Visintini et al. blog post makes this point
directly: AI-generated code that scored 77% on their test was flagged by
a Stata expert as unnecessarily complex, with elaboration that obscured
rather than clarified analytical intent. Producing working code is not
the same as producing maintainable, research-grade code. The skill of
recognizing the difference is precisely what a good analyst needs.

From the course experience and the evolving landscape, several skills
are becoming essential for analysts who use AI effectively:

- **Context engineering.** Structuring your projects, instructions, and
  skills files so the AI has the information it needs — and does not
  waste context on information it does not.
- **Prompt discipline.** Writing clear, scoped requests that reduce
  hallucination and keep the agent focused. Understanding when to ask AI
  to explain first and act second.
- **Code review and validation.** Reading AI-generated diffs critically.
  Catching unnecessary complexity, redundant logic, or deviations from
  established coding standards.
- **Version control.** Git is no longer optional when an autonomous
  agent edits your files. The ability to review changes line by line,
  revert, and accept selectively is fundamental.
- **Domain judgment.** Knowing what the analysis should produce.
  Understanding the data, the econometrics, the policy context — the
  parts that no model can replace.
- **Tool orchestration.** Choosing and configuring the right MCP
  servers, skills files, and agent configurations for the task at hand.

DIME's own hiring process is evolving along these lines. Their next
recruitment round will ask candidates to submit AI chat transcripts
alongside their code, evaluating not only the final script but how tools
were used and corrected during the task. The assessment question is
shifting from "can you code?" to "can you use AI to produce
research-grade code?"

## Conclusion

The productivity frontier in AI-assisted coding is no longer "AI versus
no AI." It is "chat-based AI versus IDE-integrated agentic AI." The
evidence — from controlled tests, from training hundreds of colleagues,
from daily practice — consistently shows that the execution environment
is the multiplier.

The demand from XXX colleagues who joined the February seminar and April
course signals that the organization is ready. The tools — Positron,
GitHub Copilot, MCP servers, skills files — are available now. What
remains is sustained investment in both the tooling and the skills to
use it well. The returns compound.
